## [00:00 - 03:02]

Hi, this is Don Crowley. Let's talk about using features in your webinar. So we've done the product mission, the benefits, the second level benefits. Now it's finally time to talk about what most people start off talking about, which is what's in your program. So features, remember, is what your product is. So I already showed you this. So maybe five modules, it teaches you blank. Here's tools, there's videos, there's recordings, there's coaching calls, there's course, there's a course portal, maybe there's ever green access and exercises and transcripts and private members, network and quizzes and progress tracker. All these things are features. And the important thing is this. You want to briefly describe the features of your course with the operational word here being briefly. Your entire course description should never take more than five minutes, half that if possible. The key is not what's in each module or element. And so that long list of all the features I just gave you, that's not what I want you to go through. What I really want you to go through is what they accomplish as a result of each of those things that's in your course. So even in the features section of your presentation is less features and it's more benefits, actually. So let me give you an example of what this might look like. Module three shows you how to overcome the number one issue everyone has with mindfulness, how to handle it when your mind won't be quiet and keeps injecting thoughts into your meditation. So you can quickly and without stress acknowledge those thoughts, move them off the stage of your mind and continue on with your mindfulness. I didn't tell them that there's a call. I didn't tell them all those things. I simply said module three shows you how to blank so that you can blank. That's the level of description that really works in selling a program. And speaking of selling programs, hey, let's look at this from a software standpoint. So maybe this feature, so let's say you sell a piece of software scans a bank of over 50 public and private databases, identifies those that fit the profile you've established, then grabs a LinkedIn profiles for each so you can easily start a conversation that will lead to a sell. Same kind of thing. This doesn't work just for courses and those kinds of things. It works for anything. You describe briefly what the feature does and then you tell them what it does for them. Bonus tip. Don't make the number of hours in your program a feature. It used to be

## [03:02 - 04:12]

that we'd say, oh, you got 30 hours of training. It's like, unless you're telling them that you're actually stripping out the extraneous hours, condensing a 20 hour program down into 10, you don't want to talk about the number of hours in your program because lots of hours is no longer a selling point in today's world. In fact, shorter hours is actually what the selling point is today. So I suggest you don't come in emphasizing how many hours your program is because people really don't want lots anymore. They just want the good stuff. So your objective here is to give them enough information to justify the emotional decision that they've made to buy. Think of it this way. They watch your webinar and they're emotionally sold on buying. Now they have to explain and justify that purchase to their spouse, their partner, their boss, and by the way to themselves when they start getting second thoughts. And what are they going to say to that person? That's your feature list. And that's what you see in this feature section of the webinar.
